title: China’s Position on Russia’s Invasion of Ukraine
description: Key Events and Statements Summarizing China’s Position on Russia’s Invasion of Ukraine from February 21, 2022 onward.
text:
Key Events and Statements from February 21, 2022 through January 31, 2026
Below is a list of key actions and statements summarizing China’s official position on Russia’s unprovoked invasion of Ukraine, which began on February 24, 2022. Items highlighted include China’s official government statements, press conferences, messages to the international community, media publications, and where available, leaked internal Chinese Communist Party (CCP) guidance for media and propaganda outlets.
(For a Timeline of key events in the lead up to the February 21, 2022, Russian invasion of Ukraine, visit https://www.uscc.gov/research/china-russia-interactions-leading-invasion-ukraine)
Entries are organized into the following categories:
[Action]: Chinese government activity which impacts the conflict in Ukraine
[Sanctions]: Chinese efforts to undermine international sanctions on Russia
[Statement]: Statements by Chinese government officials on the Ukraine conflict
[Media]: Chinese media stories reflecting official positions and censorship guidelines
[Event]: Major international events involving China, Russia, and Ukraine
[Timeline in reverse order, most recent event on top]
January 29, 2026
[Statement] The Telegraphreports that China has provided Russia with $10.3 billion in technology and advanced equipment to support Moscow’s war machine. This equipment has included specialized manufacturing machines and tools to build warheads for Russia’s nuclear-capable, hypersonic Oreshnik missiles. When asked about the report, Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun says China does not seek to add fuel to the fire or take advantage of the war, and rejects blame-shifting.
January 23, 2026
[Statement] When asked to comment on the latest round of peace talks among Russia, the United States, and Ukraine in the United Arab Emirates, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun says China’s position on the Ukraine war is consistent and that negotiations are the only way to resolve the conflict.
January 21, 2026
[Action] The Financial Timesreports that, according to Western officials, a Chinese state-linked company used a Central Asian country as a proxy end-user to help a Russian defense company avoid Chinese drone component export controls.
January 14, 2026
[Media] Reutersreports that in 2025, China’s trade with Russia declined for the first time in five years, while the value of China’s crude oil imports from Russia fell by 19.6% in the first eleven months of 2025.
January 6, 2026
[Statement] When asked if China’s calls to respect Venezuela’s sovereignty apply to Ukraine’s situation, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Mao Ning restates Xi Jinping’s “four principles” for peace in Ukraine and says China has pushed for peace talks and a political resolution to the war since its first day.
December 30, 2025
[Statement] Russia Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov says Russia will review its negotiating position in talks about a potential peace deal in light of its allegation that Ukraine tried to attack Putin’s residence. In response to a question about this issue, China's Foreign Ministry Spokesperson calls for all parties to stick to its three principles of “no spillover of the battlefield, no escalation of the war, and no fanning of the flames.”
December 29, 2025
[Statement] When asked about Ukraine President Volodymyr Zelenskyy’s comment that China has “significantly increased” energy imports from Russia, China's Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Lin Jian says only that China has maintained close communication with Russia and Ukraine and consistently supported a ceasefire.
December 24, 2025
[Media] After President Zelenskyy states in a post on X that Russian strikes on energy infrastructure in Ukraine may be aided by data from Chinese satellites, China's state-run outlet Global Timesframes his statement an “old tactic” to drag China into the conflict and seek more military assistance from Europe and the United States.
December 23, 2025
[Sanctions] Ukraine’s President Volodymyr Zelenskyy’s announces upcoming sanctions targeting Chinese individuals “working with the Russian military industrial complex.” In response, China's Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Lin Jian says that China opposes unilateral sanctions and calls on Ukraine to “correct its mistake.“
December 17, 2025
[Statement] When asked about a Russian intelligence report that the UK, France, and Germany will threaten closer ties to China as leverage against a U.S.-promoted peace agreement, Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun says China supports a “peaceful resolution” to the war in Ukraine but will safeguard its own interests.
December 8, 2025
[Event] China's Foreign Minister Wang Yi and Germany’s Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul hold talks, during which Wadephul says that Germany hopes China will push for an early end to the Ukraine war and Wang reiterates China's existing position.
December 5, 2025
[Statement] French President Emmanuel Macron and General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping release a joint statement supporting a ceasefire in Ukraine. When asked if this means China will “do more” to call on Russia to end the war, Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Lin Jian says only that China will continue to promote a political settlement to the war “in its own way” and that China supports Europe’s efforts to build a “sustainable European security framework.”
December 3, 2025
[Action] China abstains from a vote on a UN General Assembly Resolution demanding Russia return all of the Ukrainian children it forcibly transferred or deported. In a statement explaining China's abstention, Counselor Teng Fei says the protection of children during armed conflict is a “humanitarian issue” and that China does not approve of its politicization.
[Statement] In response to a question about the European Commission’s proposed plan to use frozen Russian assets to fund loans to Ukraine, China's Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Lin Jian says China opposes unilateral sanctions imposed without UN Security Council authorization.
December 2, 2025
[Event] China's Foreign Minister Wang Yi and Russia’s Security Council Secretary Sergei Shoigu hold a strategic security consultation in Moscow to discuss the war in Ukraine, among other issues. According to the Chinese readout of the meeting, Wang Yi said that China supports reaching a “comprehensive and lasting” peace deal and will continue to maintain strategic communication with Russia on the matter.
November 26, 2025
[Statement] In response to a question asking for verification of claims that Russia is consulting with China on ending the war in Ukraine, China's Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Mao Ning reiterates China's stance on the importance of dialogue and negotiation but does not specifically comment on whether such consultations are taking place.
November 24, 2025
[Event] In a phone call between U.S. President Donald Trump and General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping, Xi calls for “a fair, lasting and binding peace agreement” and for a resolution to the war in Ukraine that solves “the crisis at its root.”
[Media] According to the Financial Times, Chinese exporters have been sharply raising prices for Russian military-industrial buyers as Western export controls and sanctions deepen Moscow’s dependence on Chinese suppliers.
[Statement] In her opening speech at the EU Indo-Pacific Ministerial Forum, EU High Representative Kaja Kallas states that on top of fueling Russia’s war in Ukraine, China is working with Russia “to overhaul the global economic and security order.” In response, China's Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Mao Ning says China-Russia relations have developed on the basis of “non-alignment, non-confrontation, and non-targeting of third parties.”
November 21, 2025
[Statement] When asked about reports that Ukrainian Defense Forces destroyed a Chinese-made rocket system used by Russian troops, China's Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Mao Ning states that she is not aware of the situation and that China has never provided lethal weapons to either party in the conflict.
November 20, 2025
[Statement] At a UN Security Council open briefing on Ukraine, China’s Permanent Representative to the UN Fu Cong calls on the international community to increase humanitarian assistance and refrain from actions that exacerbate the conflict, including providing weapons to either party.
November 17, 2025
[Statement] When asked about U.S. President Donald Trump’s stated support for a proposed Senate bill to sanction countries that trade with Russia, China's Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Mao Ning states that China always opposes unilateral sanctions that are not authorized by the UN Security Council or based in international law.
November 13, 2025
[Statement] The G7 foreign ministers issue a joint statement condemning China’s “provision of weapons and dual-use components” to Russia and reiterating that Beijing is a “decisive enabler” of Russia’s war in Ukraine. In response, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Lin Jian says China has never supplied lethal arms to either party, strictly controls the export of dual-use articles, and will not accept the G7 “shifting blame” for the conflict.
November 3, 2025
[Statement] When asked whether the “Friends for Peace” group led by China and Brazil has directly called on Russia to end its war in Ukraine, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Mao Ning states only that the purpose of the group is to promote a ceasefire and peace talks.
October 30, 2025
[Event] U.S. President Donald Trump says that he and General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping discussed the war in Ukraine “for a long time” at their bilateral meeting in South Korea and that the sides would “work together” on ending the war. China’s official readout of the meeting does not mention Ukraine.
October 24, 2025
[Action] In response to U.S. sanctions on Russian oil giants Rosneft and Lukoil, China’s largest state-owned oil companies suspend all imports of Russian seaborne oil.
[Statement] When asked about Ukraine’s President Volodymyr Zelenskyy’s claim that there is no permanent dialogue between Chinese and Ukrainian leaders, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun says only that China is working constantly to promote a ceasefire and peace talks.
October 23, 2025
[Statement] In response to U.S. President Donald Trump’s comment that General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping “can have a big influence on Putin,” China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun says that “coercion and pressure will not solve the problem.”
[Sanctions] In response to the EU’s 19th sanctions package against Russia for its war in Ukraine, which lists two Chinese oil refineries among other Chinese entities, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun expresses “firm opposition” to the sanctions. He further states that China will take all necessary measures to protect its “legitimate rights and interests.”
October 22, 2025
[Statement] Ukraine’s President Volodymyr Zelenskyy and numerous European leaders issue a joint statement calling for the current line of contact to be the “starting point of negotiations” to end the war in Ukraine and reiterating their position that “international borders must not be changed by force.” In response, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun states that China hopes for a lasting peace agreement.
October 20, 2025
[Media] The South China Morning Postreports that despite pressure from the United States to curb purchases of Russian oil, China’s oil imports from Russia grew 4.3 percent in September relative to the previous month.
October 17, 2025
[Statement] China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Lin Jian expresses condolences for the death of Russian reporter Ivan Zuyev, who was reportedly hit by a Ukrainian drone while reporting in Ukraine. Lin calls for journalists to be protected and reiterates that dialogue and negotiation are the only ways to resolve the crisis.
October 4, 2025
[Action] The Foreign Intelligence Service of Ukraine announces that it has “recorded evidence” that China provides Russia with satellite intelligence data for missile strikes on Ukraine, including on targets that “may belong to foreign investors.”
September 30, 2025
[Statement] At a reception hosted by the Chinese embassy in Kyiv, China’s Ambassador to Ukraine Ma Shengkun says that China continues to pursue a ceasefire and a “just and lasting peace” agreement to end the war in Ukraine and wants to work with Ukraine to expand areas of mutually beneficial cooperation.
September 26, 2025
[Statement] In a Foreign Affairs interview, Poland’s Foreign Minister Radoslaw Sikorski asserts that China is the only country that could force an end to the Ukraine war, but that the conflict’s continuation suits China’s interests. In response, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun states that it is not China but other parties that are profiting from the war and do not wish to see it end.
September 24, 2025
[Statement] In response to Ukraine’s President Volodymyr Zelenskyy’s statement at a UN Security Council meeting that “without China, Putin's Russia is nothing,” China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun says only that China has always been “objective and impartial” on the Ukraine crisis.
[Statement] When asked about U.S. President Donald Trump’s accusation at the UN General Assembly that China is a major financier of the Ukraine war through its purchases of Russian oil, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun replies that third parties should not interfere with or attempt to influence China and Russia’s trade cooperation.
September 15, 2025
[Statement] In response to the United States asking G7 and European Union leaders to impose tariffs on countries that purchase Russian oil, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Lin Jian says only that Beijing’s economic and energy cooperation with Russia is “fully legitimate and lawful” and that economic coercion will not solve the problem.
September 1, 2025
[Event] Russia’s President Vladimir Putin attends the Shanghai Cooperation Organization (SCO) Summit in Tianjin and delivers a speech repeating Russia’s narrative blaming the West for Russia’s war in Ukraine. Ukraine’s Foreign Ministry issues a statement criticizing the final declaration from the SCO meeting for failing to mention the war. Ukraine’s statement also reiterates that it welcomes “a more active role” for Beijing in ending the conflict.
August 29, 2025
[Statement] When asked about Russia’s recent strikes on Kyiv that killed more than 20 people, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun does not directly condemn the strikes, but reiterates China’s “three principles of no expansion of the battlefield, no escalation of the fighting, and no party adding fuel to the flames.”
August 28, 2025
[Sanctions] For the first time, a tanker carrying liquified natural gas from the U.S.-sanctioned Arctic LNG 2 plant in Russia docked at a Chinese terminal.
August 25, 2025
[Statement] For the first time since Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping sends a congratulatory message to Ukraine’s President Volodymyr Zelenskyy on Ukraine’s Independence Day. While Zelenskyy posted Xi’s message on social media, China’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs declined to confirm whether Xi had sent the message.
August 20, 2025
[Statement] Ukraine’s President Volodymyr Zelenskyy states that he does not consider China to be a potential security guarantor for a peace deal with Russia due to China’s lack of support for Ukraine and its ongoing supply of drones to Russia. In response, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Mao Ning says that China remains willing to play a “constructive role” in promoting a resolution to the war.
August 13, 2025
[Action] In response to new EU sanctions on two Chinese banks for providing “crypto asset services” to Russia, China’s Ministry of Commerce announces retaliatory sanctions on two Lithuanian banks, UAB Urbo Bankas and AB Mano Bankas.
August 8, 2025
[Sanctions] In response to commentsby U.S. President Donald Trump that the United States could impose additional tariffs on China for purchasing sanctioned Russian oil, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun says that all of China’s trade with Russia is “legitimate and legal.”
[Statement] During a call with General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping, Russia’s President Vladimir Putin shares Russia’s views on the current state of the Ukraine crisis and recent discussions with the United States. Xi says that China is pleased to see ongoing U.S.-Russia communication and efforts to promote a political settlement of the Ukraine crisis.
August 6, 2025
[Statement] In a call with Chief Advisor to the President of Brazil Celso Amorim, China’s Foreign Minister Wang Yi states that discussions on ending Russia’s war in Ukraine are at a critical moment. Wang Yi further says that China and Brazil can continue playing a role in promoting a political resolution to the crisis and building consensus among “Global South” countries in favor of a ceasefire.
July 27, 2025
[Sanctions] Ukraine’s President Volodymyr Zelenskyy sanctions 53 individuals and entities accused of supporting Russia’s defense industry, including some Chinese nationals and companies. China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun criticizes the sanctions and urges Ukraine to “correct its mistake immediately.”
July 25, 2025
[Event] The UN Security Council holds a briefing during which the U.S. and Chinese representatives exchange recriminations regarding Russia’s war in Ukraine. Acting U.S. Representative to the UN Dorothy Shea urges China to stop providing Russia with dual-use goods that enable its drone and missile attacks in Ukraine. In response, China’s Deputy Ambassador to the UN Geng Shuang criticizes the United States for “spreading lies” about China and “creating divisions” on the Ukraine issue.
July 23, 2025
[Sanctions] According toReuters, Chinese companies are covertly shipping engines to Russian state-owned weapons manufacturer IEMZ Kupol by falsely labeling them as “industrial refrigeration units” to evade Western sanctions. These shipments have enabled IEMZ Kupol to increase production of its Garpiya-A1 attack drone, which has been used to attack military and civilian targets in Ukraine.
July 13, 2025
[Statement] On the sidelines of the Shanghai Cooperation Organization meeting in Beijing, China’s Foreign Minister Wang Yi meets with Russia’s Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov to exchange views on the war in Ukraine, among other issues.
July 9, 2025
[Action] The Security Service of Ukraine arrests two Chinese nationals, a father and son, for allegedly spying on Ukraine’s Neptune anti-ship missile program, which is critical to Ukraine’s coastal defense against the Russian navy. China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Mao Ning states that China is “still confirming the relevant information” and will “protect the legal rights and interests of Chinese citizens.”
July 2, 2025
[Statement] China’s Foreign Minister Wang Yi tells European Union officials that Beijing does not want a Russian loss in Ukraine because the United States might then shift its entire focus toward China. In a subsequent press conference, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Mao Ning states that a prolonged conflict in Ukraine is not in any party’s interests.
[Sanctions] According to the New York Times, at least 130 Chinese and Hong Kong-listed companies are advertising restricted chips for sale in Russia, including ones that are critical components in Russian cruise missiles that have been used against Ukraine.
June 26, 2025
[Event] A Chinese war correspondent with China’s Phoenix Television news outlet is injured in a Ukrainian drone attack on Russia’s Kursk region. China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun expresses deep concern for injuries suffered by Chinese journalists and calls for all sides to work toward a political resolution to the Ukraine crisis.
[Statement] In response to NATO Chief Mark Rutte calling for more NATO expenditures in light of China, Iran, and North Korea’s support for Russia’s war in Ukraine, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun criticizes NATO for spreading “false information” and says that if NATO cares about European and global security, it should stop “adding fuel to the fire” and “instigating confrontation.”
[Statement] In response to a question about Ukrainian reports stating that Russia plans to host a late-2025 training for about 600 Chinese military personnel on how to counter Western weapons based on Russia’s experiences during its war in Ukraine, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun says that he has no information to share.
June 20, 2025
[Event] In a speech at a UN Security Council briefing on Ukraine, China’s Deputy Permanent Representative to the UN Geng Shuang says that China welcomes the progress made during Russia and Ukraine’s recent peace talks in Istanbul and encourages the two sides to hold another round of negotiations, quoting the Chinese proverb that “three-foot-thick ice does not form overnight.”
June 19, 2025
[Action] Chinese hacking groups with ties to Beijing have repeatedly targeted Russian government agencies and defense corporations to gain information about the weapons and tactics deployed in Russia’s war in Ukraine, according to a New York Times report based on assessments by analysts at numerous cybersecurity firms.
June 12, 2025
[Sanctions] According to a report by Bloomberg, the European Union has proposed sanctioning two Chinese banks for helping Russia evade trade restrictions imposed after its invasion of Ukraine. In response, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Lin Jian says that China opposes all unilateral sanctions that have not been approved by the UN Security Council and that dealings between Chinese and Russian companies conform to World Trade Organization rules.
June 7, 2025
[Action] According to a New York Times article, an internal Russian intelligence document written in late 2023 or early 2024 claimed that China was spying on Russian military operations in Ukraine to learn more about Western weapons and tactics. When asked to comment on the report, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Lin Jian says that he was not aware of the situation.
May 31, 2025
[Statement] In a keynote address at the Shangri-La Dialogue in Singapore, French President Emmanuel Macron warns against “double standards,” asking what could happen in Taiwan and the Philippines if “Russia could be allowed to take a part of the territory of Ukraine without any restriction,” and emphasizing that what is at stake in Ukraine is “our common credibility” to “preserve territorial integrity and sovereignty of people.” In response, the Chinese Embassy in Singapore criticizes the comparison “as unacceptable” in a post on Facebook, stating that “using a double standard to look at a double standard still results in a double standard.”
May 27, 2025
[Sanctions] In an interview with Ukraine’s national news agency, the head of Ukraine’s intelligence agency, Oleh Ivashchenko, states that Ukraine has “confirmed data” that China has provided special chemicals, gunpowder, and components specifically designed for military purposes to 20 Russian military factories. China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Mao Ning denies the allegations, stating that China has never provided lethal weapons to Russia or Ukraine and “strictly controls” dual-use items.
May 13, 2025
[Statement] During a state visit to Beijing alongside the fourth ministerial meeting of the China-CELAC (Community of Latin American and Caribbean States) Forum, Brazil’s President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva and General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping release a joint statement on the crisis in Ukraine. The statement welcomes Russia’s May 10 proposal for peace talks with Ukraine and states that a solution to the conflict must resolve “the reasonable concerns of all parties” and the “roots” of the conflict.
May 9, 2025
[Action] The Chinese People’s Liberation Army participates in a military parade in Moscow to commemorate the eightieth anniversary of the end of World War II, which Ukraine’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs said it regarded as a violation of neutrality in the Russia-Ukraine conflict. China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Lin Jian stated that Chinese participation in the parade was meant to express China’s “respect for history” and determination to uphold the legacy of victory in World War II.
[Statement] During a state visit to Moscow timed to coincide with Russia’s Victory Day Parade, General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping and Russia’s President Vladimir Putin release a joint statement that adopts Russia’s preferred language that a solution to the war in Ukraine must “eliminate the root causes of the conflict” and “take into account the legitimate security interests and concerns of all countries.”
April 29, 2025
[Statement]: At a meeting of the United Nations Security Council on the Ukraine crisis, China’s Deputy Permanent Representative to the UN Geng Shuang states that key stakeholders should cooperate “to address the root causes of the crisis and to reach a peace agreement” and reiterates that China’s position on the conflict in Ukraine “is objective, impartial, rational, and pragmatic.”
April 28, 2025
[Statement]: In response to multiple media questions about whether China will pressure North Korea to stop sending troops to fight for Russia as requested by Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun responds only that China has already stated its stance on this issue multiple times and maintains a “consistent and clear” position on the Ukraine crisis.
April 22, 2025
[Event]: Chinese Ambassador to Ukraine Ma Shengkun meets with Ukraine’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs at the latter’s request. According to Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy, the Ukrainian government called the meeting to discuss concerns over China’s involvement in the war. In response, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun says that China “firmly opposes groundless accusations and political manipulation.”
April 18, 2025
[Statement] Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy accuses China of supplying the Russian military with gunpowder and artillery and announces sanctions on three Chinese companies: Beijing Aviation and Aerospace Xianghui Technology Co. Ltd, Rui Jin Machinery Co. Ltd, and Zhongfu Shenying Carbon Fiber Xining Co. Ltd. China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Lin Jian states in response that China has never provided lethal weapons to either party and that Ukraine itself has publicly acknowledged that “Russia imports most weapons components from the United States and other Western countries.”
April 15, 2025
[Statement]: Two Chinese mercenaries captured by the Ukrainian military, Zhang Renbo and Wang Guangjun, publicly declare their desire to be returned to China despite knowing they will likely face punishment for fighting in the Russian military. In response to media questions on the subject, Chinese Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Lin Jian reiterates that China has repeatedly warned its citizens to avoid armed conflicts abroad and says that China will handle cases involving Chinese citizens overseas “in accordance with the law.”
April 10, 2025
[Statement]: In response to Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy’s assertion that 155 Chinese nationals are fighting for Russia against Ukraine and that China “knew about it,” China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Lin Jian criticizes Zelenskyy’s remarks as “irresponsible” and calls upon Ukraine to “correctly and clear-headedly” recognize China’s role in promoting a peaceful solution to the conflict.
April 9, 2025
[Statement]: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy announces that Ukrainian troops have captured two Chinese citizens fighting for the Russian military in Ukraine, marking the first time either side has taken Chinese nationals as prisoners since the war broke out. In response, Chinese Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Lin Jian says that the government is verifying the situation with Ukrainian counterparts and states that the Chinese government “always asks Chinese nationals to avoid areas of armed conflict, avoid any form of involvement in armed conflict, and especially to avoid participation in any party’s military operations.”
March 31, 2025
[Statement] In response to comments made by U.S. President Donald Trump in which he threatened secondary tariffs on any country that purchased Russian oil if Russia blocked progress on a peace deal in Ukraine, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun states that Sino-Russian cooperation “does not target any third party and is not influenced by any factor from any third party.”
March 26, 2025
[Statement] China’s Representative to the United Nations Fu Cong delivers remarks to the United Nations Security Council regarding Russia’s war in Ukraine. Fu states that a “window of peace” is opening in Ukraine and that China welcomes the recent negotiations in Riyadh between the United States and Russia and the United States and Ukraine. He emphasizes the importance of listening to “the voices of the Global South” regarding the repercussions of the conflict in Ukraine and praises the work of the “Friends for Peace” group led by China and Brazil.
March 22, 2025
[Statement] According to reporting by the German newspaper Die Welt, Chinese diplomats have reached out to their European counterparts in Brussels to discuss whether Chinese peacekeepers could be included as part of a larger peacekeeping force that would help enforce a peace deal in Ukraine. China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun stated that the report is “entirely untrue.”
[Event] China’s Representative to the United Nations Fu Cong and Brazil’s Representative to the United Nations Sérgio França Danese lead a meeting of the “Friends for Peace” group in New York City to discuss recent developments in the war in Ukraine. Noting that the conflict might be “approaching a turning point,” they call for an immediate ceasefire and for all stakeholders to “embrace this opportunity” to engage in peace talks.
March 19, 2025
[Statement] At a Chinese Foreign Ministry press conference, a reporter from Sweden’s national public television broadcaster requests comment on Chinese companies’ oil exploration and mining activities in Russian-occupied territory in Ukraine, which the reporter claims violate international humanitarian law, the 1949 Geneva Convention, and the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea. In response, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Mao Ning states that she is not aware of the circumstances mentioned. Ukrainian media outlets have reported that Chinese companies in the energy, agriculture, and light industry sectors have been actively operating in Russian-occupied Ukrainian territories.
March 17, 2025
[Statement] In response to a statement made by the G7 Foreign Ministers criticizing China’s “provision of weapons and dual-use components” for Russia’s war in Ukraine, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Mao Ning states that China “has never provided lethal weapons to either side in the conflict” and “absolutely rejects the G7’s blame-shifting.”
February 25, 2025
[Statement] In response to questions about new sanctions imposed by Canada, the European Union, and the United Kingdom on Chinese entities that supply dual-use goods to Russia for use in its war against Ukraine, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Lin Jian states that China “opposes all unilateral sanctions that have not been approved by the UN Security Council,” strictly controls exports of dual-use goods, and will take “necessary measures” to protect the “lawful rights and interests” of Chinese companies.
February 24, 2025­
[Action] On the third anniversary of Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, China votes in favor of a U.S.-authored UN Security Council Resolution calling for a “swift end to the conflict.” In a speech explaining China’s vote in favor of the resolution, China’s Ambassador to the United Nations Fu Cong reiterates that China “supports the consensus reached by the United States and Russia on starting peace talks” and hopes that all stakeholders can participate in peace talks “at the appropriate time.” Additionally, China abstains on a UN General Assembly resolution introduced by Ukraine calling for Russia to immediately and unconditionally withdraw all military forces from Ukraine.
[Action] General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping holds a phone call with Russia’s President Vladimir Putin to discuss Russia’s war in Ukraine, among other issues. According to the Chinese readout of the call, Putin provided updates on Russia’s latest contacts with the United States regarding the war in Ukraine and reiterated Russia’s hope for a peace deal that “removes the root causes” of the conflict. Xi emphasized China and Brazil’s efforts to work with “Global South” countries to create the conditions for a political settlement to the war and reiterated that China is glad to see Russia working with “relevant parties” to resolve the crisis.
February 20, 2025
[Statement] In his address to the G20 Foreign Ministers’ Meeting in Johannesburg, South Africa, China’s Foreign Minister Wang Yi states that a “window of opportunity” is opening for peace in Ukraine and reiterates China’s support for "all efforts dedicated to peace,” including “the recent consensus reached between the United States and Russia.” Asked whether Wang Yi’s failure to reiterate China’s frequent assertion that “all stakeholders” should be involved in peace talks indicated a change in China’s position, Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun responded only that China’s position on the Ukraine crisis is “consistent and clear.”
[Statement] China’s Foreign Minister Wang Yi meets with Russia’s Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov on the sidelines of the G20 Foreign Ministers’ Meeting. According to the Chinese readout of the meeting, Lavrov explained the “latest developments” in the Ukraine crisis and said that Russia is focused on solving the “root cause of the problem.” Wang Yi stated that China would continue to promote peace talks and aim to play a constructive role in finding a “political solution to the crisis.”
February 16, 2025
[Media] In an interview with German media outlet DW at the Munich Security Conference, Zhou Bo, a retired senior colonel in the Chinese military and a senior fellow at Tsinghua University, suggests that China can join “other major powers” in providing a “collective security guarantee” and contribute peacekeeping forces to support a potential peace deal between Russia and Ukraine.
February 15, 2025
[Action] China’s Foreign Minister Wang Yi meets with Ukraine’s Foreign Minister Andrii Sybiha on the sidelines of the Munich Security Conference. According to the Chinese readout of the meeting, Wang Yi stated that China “supports all efforts conducive to peace” and hopes to see a sustainable peace deal that is “acceptable to all stakeholders.” According to the Ukrainian readout, “Wang Yi emphasized that Ukraine should be a party to any possible peace talks” and both sides “reaffirmed their mutual respect for the territorial integrity of Ukraine and China.”
February 14, 2025
[Statement] Responding to questions at the Munich Security Conference, China’s Foreign Minister Wang Yi says that China supports “all efforts conducive to peace” in Ukraine, including the “consensus regarding peace talks” reached between the United States and Russia. Wang Yi further states that as the war is happening on “European soil,” Europe should play “an important role” in negotiating an end to the conflict.
February 13, 2025
[Statement] According to a report in the Wall Street Journal, Chinese officials have proposed arranging a summit between U.S. President Donald Trump and Russian President Vladimir Putin to help negotiate a peace deal to end Russia’s war in Ukraine. According to the report, Chinese officials also suggested that China could serve as a “guarantor” for a peace deal by sending peacekeeping troops to the region. In response to a question about whether China was considering sending peacekeeping forces to Ukraine, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun stated that he would not respond to “hypothetical questions.”
January 30, 2025
[Sanctions] According to a report, several Chinese companies with ties to the Chinese government are supplying Russia with gallium, germanium, and antimony, critical minerals that are used to produce drones and missiles for Russia’s war in Ukraine. China banned the export of these same minerals to the United States in December 2024, citing their military applications.
January 24, 2025
[Statement] In video remarks to the World Economic Forum in Davos, U.S. President Donald Trump states that China has “a great deal of power” over the situation in Ukraine and expresses hope that “China can help us stop the war.” In response, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Mao Ning states that China will continue to promote peace talks and is willing to maintain communication with all sides.
January 22, 2025
[Statement] China’s Foreign Minister Wang Yi holds a phone call with Celso Amorim, chief special advisor to the Brazilian president, to discuss Russia’s war in Ukraine, among other issues. According to the Chinese readout of the call, the two sides discussed China and Brazil’s “six-point peace plan” and using the “Friends for Peace” group to create consensus within the “Global South” on peace talks to end the war in Ukraine.
January 21, 2025
[Statement] General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping holds a video call with Russian President Vladimir Putin to discuss Russia’s war in Ukraine, among other issues. According to Russian foreign policy aid Yuri Ushakov, during the call Putin expressed willingness to engage with the United States on the Ukraine conflict but insisted that any deal must take Russia’s interests into account. The Chinese readout of the meeting did not explicitly mention the war in Ukraine.
January 17, 2025
[Statement] General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping holds a phone call with U.S. President-elect Donald Trump to discuss Russia’s war in Ukraine, among other issues. President Trump later told reporters that during the call he asked Xi to help “settle” the war in Ukraine. The Chinese readout only mentions that the two leaders “exchanged views” on the crisis in Ukraine.
January 16, 2025
[Statement] Speaking at a UN Security Council briefing on Ukraine, China’s Deputy Permanent Representative to the United Nations Geng Shuang reiterates China’s “three principles” of “no expansion of the battlefield, no escalation of the fighting, and no provocation by any party.” Geng denies that China sells military supplies to Russia or provides political cover for Russia’s war effort and accuses the United States of using the war to “stoke up bloc confrontation, create divisions, and contain and isolate China.”
January 13, 2025
[Statement] At a Chinese Foreign Ministry Press Conference, a reporter for the National News Agency of Ukraine states that Ukraine’s capture of two North Korean soldiers “proves that Russia and North Korea are conducting military cooperation in violation of UN Security Council sanctions on North Korea” and asks for comment. China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun said he is not aware of the specific circumstances surrounding the situation.
January 10, 2025
[Sanctions] The U.S. Department of the Treasury imposes sweeping sanctions targeting Russian oil exports that help fund Russia’s war in Ukraine, including sanctions on oil tankers that cumulatively carried approximately 300 million barrels of Russian crude to China in 2024. Treasury also added numerous Hong Kong- and PRC-based entities with ties to Russia’s energy sector to its Specially Designated Nationals (SDN) List. In response to a question about the new U.S. sanctions, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun again denounced “illegal” U.S. sanctions that have not been authorized by the UN Security Council.
January 7, 2025
[Sanctions] Amid reports that the United States is planning sanctions on Russian oil exports that help fund Russia’s War in Ukraine, China’s Shandong Port Group issues a notice prohibiting U.S.-sanctioned oil tankers from docking at its ports. Shandong province is home to many of the independent oil refineries that have been importing sanctioned Russian oil. Asked to comment, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Guo Jiakun stated that he is not aware of the specific circumstances surrounding the prohibition and reiterated that China opposes all sanctions that have not been approved by the UN Security Council.
December 23, 2024
[Event] China’s recently appointed Ambassador to Ukraine Ma Shengkun meets with Ukraine’s President Volodymyr Zelenskyy and presents his letter of credence. According to the Ukraine Office of the President’s readout of the meeting, Zelenskyy raised the importance of developing bilateral trade relations and discussed North Korea’s involvement in the war against Ukraine.
December 18, 2024
[Event] Members of the “Friends for Peace” group meet in New York at the level of Permanent Representatives to the UN to discuss Russia’s war in Ukraine. According to the press release of the meeting, representatives expressed concerns about recent escalations in the conflict, reaffirmed the principles of respect for the “sovereignty and territorial integrity” and “reasonable concerns” of all countries, and conveyed support for “Global South” countries playing a constructive role in achieving a “comprehensive and enduring” political solution to the crisis. According to China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Mao Ning, the meeting was attended by representatives of 17 countries, including China, Brazil, Algeria, South Africa, and Turkey.
December 17, 2024
[Sanctions] The Council of the European Union for the first time imposes “fully-fledged sanctions” (including a travel ban, asset freeze, and prohibition on making available economic resources) on various Chinese entities and individuals that “supply drone components and microelectronic components in support of Russia’s war of aggression in Ukraine.” In response, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Lin Jian asserts that China has the “strictest standards in the world” for export controls on drones and that it “firmly opposes” the EU’s “unreasonable sanctions on Chinese enterprises.”
December 12, 2024
[Statement] General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping meets with Deputy Chairman of Russia’s Security Council Dmitry Medvedev in Beijing to discuss Russia’s war in Ukraine, among other issues. According to the Chinese readout, Xi reiterates China’s support for the “three principles” of “no expansion of the battlefield, no escalation in the fighting, and no party adding fuel to the flames” and expresses willingness to continue working with the international community to create conditions for a political settlement to the crisis. Medvedev states that Russia positively evaluates China’s position on the conflict, has taken note of China and Brazil’s “Friends for Peace” initiative, and is willing to promote a political settlement to the crisis.
December 2, 2024
[Statement] Germany’s Foreign Minister Annalena Baerbock meets with China’s Foreign Minister Wang Yi in Beijing for the seventh round of the China-Germany Strategic Dialogue on Diplomacy and Security to discuss Russia’s war in Ukraine, among other issues. According to the Chinese readout of the meeting, Wang Yi explained China’s position in favor of promoting peace talks. According to the German readout of the meeting, Baerbock noted that “increasing Chinese support for Russia’s war against Ukraine has an impact on our relations” and affects “core German and European security interests.”
December 1, 2024
[Sanctions] Wall Street Journalreports that in the months following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, Beijing established an interagency group to study the impact of Western sanctions on Russia and analyze how to mitigate the effect of potential sanctions in the event of a conflict over Taiwan. As part of this effort, Chinese officials traveled periodically to Moscow to meet with the Russian Central Bank, Russia’s Finance Ministry, and other Russian agencies involved in countering sanctions. According to the Wall Street Journal, the interagency group reports to He Lifeng, China’s vice premier and director of the Office of the Central Financial and Economic Affairs Commission, who “has a direct line to Xi Jinping.”
November 26, 2024
[Sanctions] In response to reports that the European Union plans to sanction several Chinese firms helping Russian companies develop attack drones deployed for use in Ukraine, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Mao Ning states that China opposes unilateral sanctions that have not been approved by the UN Security Council. She further claims that China has not provided any weapons to the parties in the conflict, strictly controls the export of dual use items including civilian drones, and opposes the use of civilian drones for military purposes.
November 22, 2024
[Statement] In response to a question about Russia firing a nuclear-capable intermediate-range ballistic missile in retaliation for Ukraine’s use of long-range missiles provided by Britain and the United States, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Lin Jian states that “all sides should remain calm and exercise restraint” and calls for de-escalation through dialogue and negotiation.
November 21, 2024
[Event] China’s new ambassador to Ukraine, Ma Shengkun, presents his credentials to Ukrainian Deputy Minister of Foreign Affairs Yevhen Perebyinis and begins work in Kyiv. In a written statement, Ma expresses hope for deepened cooperation between the two countries but does not explicitly mention Russia’s war in Ukraine. Prior to arriving in Ukraine, Ma was deputy director general of China’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs Department of Arms Control.
November 20, 2024
[Statement] General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping meets with Brazil’s President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva in Brazil. According to the Chinese readout of the meeting, Brazil expressed willingness to continue closely cooperating with China to promote a peaceful resolution to the war in Ukraine.
November 19, 2024
[Statement] General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping meets separately with French President Emmanuel Macron and German Chancellor Olaf Scholz on the sidelines of the G20 Summit in Brazil to “exchange views on the Ukraine crisis,” among other issues. According to international media accounts of the meetings, both Macron and Scholz urge Xi to put greater pressure on Russia to deescalate the war in Ukraine.
November 18, 2024
[Statement] In his address at the G20 Summit in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping expresses support for “seeking a political solution to the crisis in Ukraine” and calls for the United Nations and its Security Council to play a greater role in “peacefully settling crises.”
[Statement] China’s Foreign Minister Wang Yi meets with Russia’s Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov on the sidelines of the G20 summit in Brazil. According to the Chinese readout of the meeting, “the two sides exchanged views on the Ukraine crisis and the situation on the Korean peninsula.”
[Statement] In response to reports that the European Union has “conclusive” intelligence showing that armed drones are being produced for the Russian military in China, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Lin Jian states that China “has not provided any lethal weapons” to the parties of the conflict and has “always strictly controlled military and dual-use drones in accordance with laws and regulations.” Lin criticizes the “double standards” of “some countries” that are “adding fuel to the fire” of the conflict in Ukraine.
November 17, 2024
[Media] General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping publishes a signed editorial in the Brazilian newspaper Folha de S.Paulo praising China and Brazil’s “six-point peace plan” for the political settlement of the war in Ukraine and stating that the plan “has received a positive response from the international community.”
November 16, 2024
[Statement] General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping and U.S. President Joe Biden meet in Lima, Peru during a meeting of the Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation Forum (APEC) and discuss Russia’s war in Ukraine, among other issues. Xi states that China’s position on the Ukraine conflict has always been open and candid and highlights China’s efforts to promote peace talks through shuttle diplomacy. Biden expresses “deep concern” over China’s support for Russia’s defense industrial base.
November 1, 2024
[Statement] In response to a question about the presence of North Korean troops in Russia, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Lin Jian states that although China has noted reports to this effect, it is not aware of the specific circumstances surrounding Russia and North Korea’s bilateral exchanges. Lin states that Russia and North Korea are both “independent sovereign states” and how to develop their bilateral ties is “their own matter.”
October 31, 2024
[Event] U.S. Secretary of State Antony Blinken states that U.S. and Chinese officials have engaged in “robust” conversation about North Korea’s deployment of troops to Russia to help with its war in Ukraine. According to Blinken, the United States made clear its expectation that China would use its influence with North Korea “to work to curb these activities.” To date, there has been no official government statement from China regarding these discussions.
October 30, 2024
[Event] China’s Foreign Minister Wang Yi meets with Russia’s Deputy Foreign Minister Andrei Rudenko in Beijing to “exchange views” on the war in Ukraine.
[Sanctions] The U.S. Commerce Department’s Bureau of Industry and Security announces the addition of 40 entities, including some based in China, to the Entity List for procuring high-priority U.S.-branded microelectronics on behalf of Russia. Simultaneously, four corporate addresses in Hong Kong associated with “significant transshipment of sensitive items to Russia” were also added to the Entity List.
October 24, 2024
[Statement] In response to questions about whether China believes that North Korean troops have been sent to Russia and how they would affect the war in Ukraine, China’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs Spokesperson Lin Jian states that China “is not aware of the relevant circumstances” and reiterates its call for a political solution to the conflict.
October 23, 2024
[Statement] Addressing the BRICS Summit in Kazan, Russia, General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping states that China, Brazil, and other countries from the “Global South” have launched a group of “Friends for Peace” to bring together more voices “committed to peace” with regard to Russia’s war in Ukraine. Xi also reiterates support for the concept of “indivisible security” and the “three principles” of “no spillover of the battlefield, no escalation of the war, and no fanning of the flames.”
October 17, 2024
[Sanctions] The U.S. Department of the Treasury announces sanctions on two private companies in China for making and shipping long-range attack drones to Russia for use in its war in Ukraine. According to Treasury, “these are the first U.S. sanctions imposed on PRC entities directly developing and producing complete weapons systems in partnership with Russian firms.”
October 10, 2024
[Action] In response to the United States announcing “large amounts of weapons aid to Taiwan,” China’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs announces sanctions against three U.S. companies and ten senior U.S. executives, including Skydio, the largest U.S. drone maker. According to Skydio, its only customer in Taiwan is the National Fire Agency, which is responsible for emergency medical services, fire prevention, and disaster rescue; Skydio is a supplier of drones to Ukraine. Skydio states that the sanctions will reduce its battery supply, as it is one of the few components that has not yet been moved out of China.
September 27, 2024
[Event] China’s Foreign Minister Wang Yi, Brazil’s Foreign Minister Mauro Vieira, and Brazil’s Chief Advisor to the President for Foreign Policy Celso Amorim co-chair a meeting at UN headquarters in New York to launch the “Friends for Peace” group on the Ukraine crisis, including representatives of 17 countries from the “Global South.” According to Wang Yi, the purpose of the group is to “promote a ceasefire” to “achieve a sustainable peace.”
September 25, 2024
[Event] China’s Foreign Minister Wang Yi meets with Ukraine’s Foreign Minister Andrii Sybiha in New York on the sidelines of the UN General Assembly to discuss Russia’s war in Ukraine and the broader China-Ukraine relationship. Wang Yi states that China has no “selfish interests” in the war in Ukraine and reiterates China’s support for a political settlement to the conflict.
[Sanctions] Based on documents provided by European intelligence sources, Reuters reports that a subsidiary of a Russian state-owned arms company, IEMZ Kupol, has been developing, testing, and producing long-range attack drones in China for use in Russia’s war against Ukraine.
[Action] According to Politico, a Ukrainian government document circulated among diplomats at the UN General Assembly in New York outlines a diplomatic effort by China to enlist support from countries in Asia, Africa, and Latin America for its peace plan, which includes terms favorable to Russia and could allow Russia to continue occupying Ukrainian territory.
September 12, 2024
[Event] China’s Foreign Minister Wang Yi meets with Russia’s President Vladimir Putin in St. Petersburg to discuss the war in Ukraine, among other issues. During the meeting, Putin praises the six-point peace plan put forward by China and Brazil. Separately, President of Ukraine Volodymyr Zelenskyy criticizes the six-point peace plan as “destructive” because it is biased in favor of Russia and fails to account for realities on the ground in Ukraine.
September 10, 2024
[Action/Sanctions] In comments to journalists in Brussels, U.S. Deputy Secretary of State Kurt Campbell states that China is providing Russia with materials that are “basically being applied directly to the Russian war machine.” He claims that in return for China’s “very substantial effort” to help sustain Russia’s war in Ukraine, Russia is providing China with submarine, missile, and other sensitive technologies.
September 9, 2024
[Event] Norwegian Prime Minister Jonas Gahr Støre meets with General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping in Beijing and points out that “China’s support to Russia is affecting relations between China and Europe.” During the meeting, Xi reiterates that China supports peace talks and a political solution to Russia’s war in Ukraine.
September 6, 2024
[Event] Ahead of Norwegian Prime Minister Jonas Gahr Støre’s visit to China, General Secretary of NATO Jens Stoltenberg meets with Støre in Oslo and calls on China to stop supporting Russia’s war in Ukraine, stating that “China cannot continue to fuel the largest military conflict in Europe without this impacting Beijing’s interests and reputation.”
August 29, 2024
[Event] U.S. National Security Advisor Jake Sullivan meets with General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping in Beijing and discusses Russia’s war against Ukraine among other issues.
August 27, 2024
[Event/Media] China’s Special Representative on Eurasian Affairs Li Hui holds a briefing for diplomatic envoys to China and international media to discuss the fourth round of China’s “shuttle diplomacy” on Russia’s War in Ukraine, involving visits to Brazil, South Africa, and Indonesia to promote China and Brazil’s six-point peace plan.
August 23, 2024
[Sanctions] The U.S. Departments of the Treasury and State announce new sanctions on entities and individuals that supply Russia’s military-industrial base with dual-use goods for use in Russia’s war in Ukraine, including dozens of entities based in China and Hong Kong.
[Sanctions] Simultaneously, the U.S. Commerce Department’s Bureau of Industry and Security announces the addition of 42 entities based in China and Hong Kong to the Entity list for shipping U.S.-origin items to Russia in contravention of U.S. export controls.
August 14, 2024
[Statement] China’s Special Representative on Eurasian Affairs Li Hui holds a phone call with Cardinal Matteo Zuppi, the Vatican’s special peace envoy for Ukraine, to exchange views on the conflict in Ukraine. During the call, Li promotes China and Brazil’s “six-point peace plan” as the basis for a political settlement to the war in Ukraine.
July 31, 2024
[Sanctions] China’s Ministry of Commerce announces new export control policies on drones and drone components with military applications, including infrared imaging equipment, high-precision measurement equipment, and civilian drones used for terrorism or military purposes. The new policies are scheduled to go into effect on September 1.
July 24, 2024
[Event] Ukraine’s Foreign Minister Dmytro Kuleba meets with China’s Foreign Minister Wang Yi in Guangzhou. During the meeting, Kuleba states that a “just peace” is in China’s interests and that Ukraine is open to negotiations once Russia is ready to negotiate “in good faith.”
July 10, 2024
[Statement] NATO’s Washington Summit Declaration labels China “a decisive enabler of Russia’s war against Ukraine” through its “no limits” partnership and “large-scale support for Russia’s defense industrial base.” China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Lin Jian criticizes the declaration for smearing China and claims “it is reasonable for Chinese companies to carry out normal economic and trade cooperation with countries around the world, including Russia.”
July 8, 2024
[Event] General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping meets with Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán to discuss Russia’s war in Ukraine. Xi promotes China’s position on the need for a political settlement to the war in Ukraine and claims China and Hungary “share the same basic proposition” regarding a resolution to the conflict.
July 3, 2024
[Event] General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping meets Russia’s President Vladimir Putin in Kazakhstan prior to a meeting of the Shanghai Cooperation Organization to discuss a “political settlement of the Ukrainian crisis,” among other topics.
June 27, 2024
[Statement] In response to the Leaders of the Group of Seven (G7) Communique’s promise to “take measures against actors in China” that materially support Russia’s war machine, China’s Ministry of National Defense Spokesperson Wu Jian criticizes “the United States and Western countries” for “pushing blame” for the conflict in Ukraine on China.
June 19, 2024
[Statement] U.S. Secretary of State Antony Blinken calls for China to stop supplying Russia’s defense industrial base, which has enabled Russia to continue the war. In response, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Lin Jian states that China does not provide weapons to the “warring parties” and “strictly controls” the export of dual-use items.
June 14, 2024
[Statement] Addressing the UN Security Council, China’s Deputy Permanent Representative to the UN Geng Shuang calls for immediate peace talks between Ukraine and Russia. He denies that China has played any role in instigating or supporting the war in Ukraine and insists that China has not provided lethal weapons to any party in the war.
June 12, 2024
[Sanctions] The U.S. Departments of the Treasury and State announce new sanctions on hundreds of individuals and entities, including dozens in China and Hong Kong, that have provided crucial supplies such as electronic integrated circuits, drone components, and machine tools to bolster Russia’s military-industrial base.
[Sanctions] Simultaneously, the U.S. Commerce Department’s Bureau of Industry and Security announces the addition of four entities based in China to the Entity List for their involvement in the shipment of controlled items, including drone components, to Russia for use in its war in Ukraine.
June 10, 2024
[Event] China’s Foreign Minister Wang Yi meets with Russia’s Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov in Nizhny Novgorod to discuss the conflict in Ukraine among other topics.
June 5, 2024
[Event] Andrii Sybiha, Ukraine’s first deputy minister for foreign affairs, meets with China’s Vice-Foreign Minister Sun Weidong in Beijing to “exchange views” on the conflict in Ukraine and discuss strengthening cooperation between the two countries across various fields.
June 2, 2024
[Statement] Speaking at the IISS Shangri-La Dialogue defense forum in Singapore, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy criticizes China for being “in the hands” of Russia’s President Vladimir Putin, accusing China of attempting to disrupt the planned Summit on Peace in Ukraine on Russia’s behalf and supplying Russia’s defense industry with dual-use items. In response, China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Mao Ning denies that China has pressured any countries not to attend the Summit on Peace in Ukraine scheduled to be held in Switzerland.
May 31, 2024
[Statement] China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Mao Ning confirms that China will not participate in the Summit on Peace in Ukraine, scheduled to be held in Switzerland on June 15-16, because its conditions for attending the summit were not met, including “recognition by both Russia and Ukraine, equal participation by all parties, and fair discussion of all peace plans.”
[Event] U.S. Secretary of Defense Lloyd Austin and China’s Minister of National Defense Admiral Dong Jun meet on the sidelines of the Shangri-La Dialogue in Singapore to discuss the conflict in Ukraine, among other issues.
May 23, 2024
[Statement] China’s Foreign Minister Wang Yi meets with Celso Amorim, chief advisor to Brazilian President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva, in Beijing to discuss the conflict in Ukraine. The two sides release a "six-point peace plan" calling for a political settlement to the conflict and an international peace conference recognized by both sides.
May 22, 2024
[Statement] UK Defense Secretary Grant Shapps cites new intelligence suggesting that China is sending “lethal aid to Russia” and “collaborating [with Russia] on combat equipment for use in Ukraine.” U.S. National Security Advisor Jake Sullivan states that “to date” the United States has not seen evidence of China providing weapons to Russia directly. China’s Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Wang Wenbin denies the allegations.
May 16, 2024
[Statement] General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping meets Russian President Vladimir Putin in Beijing. Xi and Putin sign a joint statement criticizing the United States for its “policy of dual containment” against China and Russia. The statement expresses Russia’s support for China’s “objective and fair” stance on the war in Ukraine as well as both China and Russia’s position that any resolution to the conflict must adhere to the principle of “indivisible security” and take into account the “legitimate security interests” of all sides.
May 10, 2024
[Statement] China’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs releases a statement describing China’s third round of shuttle diplomacy on Russia’s war in Ukraine, which took place from May 3 to 9. In this round, China’s Special Representative on Eurasian Affairs Li Hui visited Turkey, Egypt, Saudi Arabia, and the UAE to promote China’s proposals for a political settlement to the war in Ukraine.
May 9, 2024
[Sanctions] The U.S. Commerce Department’s Bureau of Industry and Security adds 37 entities based in China to the Entity List, including several that “have been involved in the shipment of controlled items to Russia following its invasion of Ukraine in February 2022.”
May 7, 2024
[Statement] General Secretary of the CCP Xi Jinping participates in a joint press conference with French President Emmanuel Macron in Paris. Xi says that he would support an international peace conference only if it were recognized by both Ukraine and Russia. Macron calls on China to curtail delivery of dual-use goods to Russia.
April 26, 2024
[Statement] U.S. Secretary of State Antony Blinken meets with China’s General Secretary Xi Jinping and other senior Chinese officials in Beijing and raises concerns about China’s support to Russia’s defense industrial base. In response to U.S. concerns, China’s Foreign Ministry spokesperson urges the United States to “stop scapegoating China and make real efforts to find a political end to the Ukraine crisis.”
April 16, 2024
[Statement] German Chancellor Olaf Scholz meets with General Secretary Xi Jinping in Beijing where the two sides discuss Ukraine. During the meeting, Xi reportedly reaffirms “four principles” that restate China’s previous positions for the restoration of peace in Ukraine.
April 15, 2024
[Sanctions] Reutersreports that the Russian Copper Company (RCC) and Chinese firms have disguised the trade of copper wire between Russia and China as scrap metal to avoid taxes and in an attempt to avoid reputational harm in light of recent sanctions prohibiting imports of Russian copper into the United States and United Kingdom. Chinese customs data shows China has imported significantly more copper scrap from Russia since December 2023.
April 12, 2024
[Sanctions] A report by the Financial Timescites senior U.S. officials saying China supplied 90 percent of microchips imported by Russia in 2023, and that these microchips were in tanks, missiles, and aircraft. The U.S. officials add that China is helping Russia to improve its satellite and space-based capabilities and providing satellite imagery to help Russia conduct war in Ukraine. Chinese entities are also “likely providing” Russia with nitrocellulose, a substance used to make propellants for weapons. The Chinese embassy in the United States disputes these claims and insists that China has “not provided weaponry to any party.”
April 9, 2024
[Statement] Russia’s foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov holds a press conference with China’s Foreign Minister Wang Yi following talks in Beijing. Minister Lavrov says Russia is “grateful to [its] Chinese friends for their unbiased, balanced position” on Ukraine. According to China’s Foreign Ministry’s spokesperson, Minister Wang said that China “hopes to see an early ceasefire in Ukraine” and supports an international peace conference recognized by both Russia and Ukraine.
April 6, 2024
[Statement] U.S. Treasury Secretary Janet L. Yellen meets with China’s Vice Premier He Lifeng in Guangzhou, China, where she warns of significant consequences if China’s companies provide material support for Russia’s war against Ukraine.
April 1, 2024
[Statement] French Minister for Europe and Foreign Affairs Stéphane Séjourné meets with China’s Foreign Minister Wang Yi in Beijing where the two sides discuss Ukraine. During a press conference, Minister Séjourné says China plays a key role in upholding the respect of international law in regards to Ukraine’s sovereignty and that France expects “China [to] send very clear messages to Russia.”
March 22, 2024
[Statement] China’s Special Representative of the Chinese Government on Eurasian Affairs Li Hui holds a press briefing for Chinese and foreign media and foreign diplomats in China in which he describes his second round of “shuttle diplomacy” on the war in Ukraine. He reiterates China’s support for the convening of an international peace conference that is recognized by both Russia and Ukraine, with equal participation from all parties. He claims that Russia, Ukraine, and relevant European countries all “expect China to play a greater constructive role” on Ukraine.
March 18, 2024
[Statement] China’s Ambassador to Switzerland Wang Shihting says in an interview that China is considering the possibility of participating in the Ukraine peace conference that Switzerland will host in June. When asked how to prevent the war in Ukraine from further escalating, Wang says that direct dialogue between Ukraine and Russia should be resumed as quickly as possible, and he indirectly condemned the “unilateral sanctions” imposed by the United States and others on Russia.
March 14, 2024
[Statement] China’s Ambassador to Ukraine Fan Xianrong meets with Ukraine President’s chief of staff Andriy Yermak to discuss Ukraine’s preparations for a proposed peace summit to be hosted in Switzerland in June.
March 11, 2024
[Statement] China’s Special Representative for Eurasian Affairs Li Hui meets with Deputy Director General for Political Affairs and Security of the Ministry for Europe and Foreign Affairs of France, Jonathan Lacôte, in Paris, where the two sides discuss the war in Ukraine. Li says the “most urgent task now is to cool down the situation and find a way out for a ceasefire and an end to the fighting as soon as possible.”
March 9, 2024
[Statement] China’s Special Representative for Eurasian Affairs Li Hui meets with State Secretary of the Federal Foreign Office of Germany Thomas Bagger in Berlin, where the two sides discussed Ukraine and the potential for peace talks, among other topics.
March 7, 2024
[Statement] China’s Special Representative for Eurasian Affairs Li Hui meets with Andriy Yermak, chief of staff to Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy in Kyiv. According to a readout by the Office of the President of Ukraine, the two sides discuss “the possibility of China’s assistance in putting an end to the forced deportation of Ukrainian children, in the exchange of prisoners, demilitarization and de-occupation of the Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Power Plant, guaranteeing nuclear security, and other international initiatives of [Ukraine].” China’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs did not offer further details on China’s response in its official readout.
[Statement] China’s Foreign Minister Wang Yi comments on China’s position on Ukraine at a press conference on the sidelines of China’s annual plenary sessions of the National People’s Congress and the Chinese People’s Political Consultative Conference known as the “Two Sessions.” He states China’s support for an international peace conference recognized by both Russia and Ukraine that would ensure the “equal participation of all parties.” He also emphasizes a need to properly address the “legitimate concerns” of all parties in order to establish a “balanced, effective, and sustainable security architecture” in Europe.
March 6, 2024
[Statement] China’s Special Representative for Eurasian Affairs Li Hui meets with Poland’s Secretary of State of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs Władysław Teofil Bartoszewsk, where the two sides discuss Ukraine. Li states China’s willingness to “promote peace talks” and to work with Poland and other countries to “cool down the situation and restore peace at an early date.”
March 4, 2024
[Statement] China’s Special Representative for Eurasian Affairs Li Hui meets with officials from the European External Action Service in Brussels to discuss the war in Ukraine and ways to promote peace. Li reiterates China’s opposition to the EU including Chinese enterprises on its list of sanctions against Russia. The EU side urges China to take steps “within its jurisdiction to curb the flow” of items being exported from China to Russia’s military industrial complex.
March 2, 2024
[Statement] China’s Special Representative for Eurasian Affairs Li Hui meets with Russian Deputy Foreign Minister Mikhail Galuzin in Moscow where the two sides discuss the possibility of a negotiated settlement to the war in Ukraine. A statement published by the Russian Foreign Ministry reportedly argues that China and Russia say it is “impossible” to discuss a Ukraine settlement without Moscow’s participation.
February 27, 2024
[Statement] China’s Vice Minister of Foreign Affairs Sun Weidong meets with Russia’s Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov in Russia where the two sides discuss Ukraine and the Korean Peninsula.
February 25, 2024
[Statement] China’s Ambassador to the UN Zhang Jun criticizes the United States, the EU, and the UK for sanctioning Chinese companies and forusing “the Ukraine crisis as a pretext.” He claims “China play[s] no part in the creation of the Ukraine crisis, nor is China party to the crisis itself.”
February 23, 2024
[Sanctions] U.S. Department of Commerce adds eight entities from China to its Entity List for activities supporting Russia’s defense-industrial sector and war effort. Five of the eight entities allegedly facilitated the diversion of controlled microelectronics to Russia’s military and intelligence authorities, while two others allegedly procured U.S.-origin machine tools, electronics test equipment, and machine tool spare parts for Russian end-users.
[Sanctions] The European Union approves its 13th package of sanctions in response to Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. Sanctions includes three Chinese firms and one Hong Kong-based company due to their role in trading electronic components of EU-origin products to Russia.
February 22, 2024
[Sanctions] The UK announces sanctions targeting individuals and businesses sustaining Russia’s war in Ukraine, including three Chinese companies. According to the UK Government, China’s Finder Technology and Juhang Aviation Technology allegedly supplied sanctioned electronics to Russia, and Beijing Micropilot Flight Control Systems’ unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) engines were found in UAVs used by Russian forces in Ukraine.
February 17, 2024
[Statement] China’s Minister of Foreign Affairs Wang Yi meets with Ukrainian Foreign Minister Dmytro Kuleba on the side lines of the Munich Security Conference. Wang Yi reportedly told Kuleba that China will “not add fuel to the fire, take advantage of opportunities to reap gains or sell lethal weapons in conflict zones or parties.”
February 16, 2024
[Statement] China’s Minister of Foreign Affairs Wang Yi meets with U.S. Secretary of State Antony Blinken on the margins of the Munich Security Conference, where the two sides exchanges views on Ukraine. Secretary Blinken raises U.S. concern of China’s support for Russia and its defense industrial base amidst Russia’s ongoing war against Ukraine.
January 31, 2024
[Statement] In his first international engagement, China’s newly appointed Minister of National Defense Admiral Dong Jun held a video call with Russian Defense Minister General Sergei Shoigu. A video of Admiral Dong’s remarks released by Russia’s Ministry of Defense shows Admiral Dong affirming Chinese support for Russia “even though the [United States] and Europe continue to put pressure on the Chinese side” over Ukraine.
January 17, 2024
[Action] Chinese officials did not meet with Ukrainian officials to discuss potential peace talks despite the fact that both China’s Premier Li Qiang and Ukraine’s President Volodymyr Zelenskyy attended the World Economic Forum in Davos. Prior to Davos, Ukraine’s presidential chief of staff Andriy Yermak expresses the importance of China’s involvement in further meetings on Ukraine’s peace formula.
January 10-12, 2024
[Statement] China’s Minister of the International Liaison Department Liu Jianchao meets with the U.S. Secretary of State Antony Blinken on January 12 and the U.S. Principal Deputy National Security Advisor Jon Finer on January 10 in Washington DC, where the two sides reportedly exchanged views on Russia’s war against Ukraine. (Note: Liu made public comments on China’s position at a 9 January event, noted below.)
January 11, 2024
[Sanctions] The Kyiv School of Economics releases a joint study with the Yermak-McFaul International Working Group on Russian Sanctions that details how Russia continues to import components for military production despite current export controls. According to the study, supply chains for battlefield goods and critical components shifted in large part because of current export controls. Russia now imports most of these goods from China.
January 9, 2024
[Statement] During a public event on January 9, held by the Council of Foreign Relations in New York, China’s Minister of the International Liaison Department Liu Jianchao defends China’s position on Russia’s war in Ukraine. He says that while China “believes that the sovereignty and territorial integrity of a country must be respected,” in the context of the war in Ukraine, “the security concern [of Russia] must also be very well addressed.” He claims that when China talks to the Russians, “they [the Russians] are showing enthusiasm [for] having peace talks with Ukraine.”
December 30, 2023
[Statement] Following two UN Security Council emergency meetings convened to discuss Russia’s December 29 missile and drone attacks against Ukraine, China’s Deputy Permanent Representative to the UN Geng Shuang calls for an early end to hostilities and encourages peace talks to positively contribute to a “political settlement of the Ukraine crisis.”
December 20, 2023
[Sanctions] Ukraine’s National Agency on Corruption Prevention includes the China Railway Construction Corporation (CRCC) on its list of “international war sponsors” for its continued construction of subways, bridges, and highways in Russia.
December 19, 2023
[Statement] China’s Ambassador to Russia Zhang Hanhui says in an interview with Russian-state-owned news agency Sputnik that energy trade between China and Russia will “not be interfered [with] by third parties.”
December 12, 2023
[Sanction] The U.S. Department of Treasury designates a network of entities and individuals based in China, Russia, Hong Kong, and Pakistan for their involvement in the procurement and facilitation of Chinese-manufactured weapons and technologies to Russia. The Treasury sanctions Chinese national Hu Xiaoxun and his China-based private defense company Jarvis H K Co., Ltd. for coordinating a network of associates to circumvent U.S. sanctions and Chinese controls on the export of military-related materials. Hu’s company allegedly transferred funds for ammunition, loitering munitions, and semiconductor microchip manufacturing equipment. Treasury also designates 7 Chinese entities for shipments to Russia that support its war efforts in Ukraine such as providing high-resolution observation satellite imagery, advanced electronics, electronic components, and technological equipment and machine tools.
December 7, 2023
[Statement] The President of the European Council Charles Michel and President of the European Commission Ursula von der Leyen travel to Beijing to attend the 24th EU-China summit where the leaders discuss the war in Ukraine with China’s President Xi Jinping and Premier Li Qiang. The EU calls on China to prevent Russia from circumventing sanctions, refrain from supplying lethal weapons to Russia, and engage with Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy’s 10-point peace formula. China's readout of Xi’s meetings did not provide additional details of China’s response to the EU’s requests.
[Sanctions] Data provided by China’s General Administration of Customs shows China-Russia trade reached $218.2 billion between January and November 2023, surpassing the total for all of 2022.
December 3, 2023
[Sanction] Russia’s military purchases 500 Chinese Desertcross 1000-3 utility terrain vehicles manufactured by Shandong Odes Industry. Russian news Agency TASS reports that Russia’s military already has around 537 basic models of the Chinese-made vehicles in service and plans to procure another 1000 in the first quarter of 2024. Russian Defense Minister Sergei Shoigu notes the vehicles are “extremely in demand.”
November 29, 2023
